title: System Lockout — Ignition Failure
shortDescription: The furnace has locked out after multiple failed ignition attempts.
fullDescription: One red flash on a Trane or American Standard furnace indicates a system lockout due to failed ignition. The control board tried to light the burners the maximum number of times without success. The system will auto-reset after a waiting period or can be manually reset by cycling power.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: If you smell gas, leave immediately and call your gas company. Do not continue to reset the furnace if it repeatedly fails to ignite.

causes.cause: Weak or failed hot surface igniter
Gas valve issue or low gas pressure

symptoms: One red LED flash on control board
Clicking or humming during ignition attempts
Furnace cycles through startup but never lights

fixSteps.title: Reset the System
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the furnace power switch, wait 30 seconds, turn it back on. Watch the ignition sequence.

fixSteps.title: Clean Flame Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and clean the flame sensor rod with fine emery cloth. Reinstall and test.

fixSteps.title: Check Igniter
fixSteps.instruction: Observe if the igniter glows during the ignition sequence. Test resistance with a multimeter (10-200 ohms is typical).

fixSteps.title: Verify Gas Supply
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the gas shutoff valve near the furnace is fully open. Verify other gas appliances work.

whenToCallPro: If cleaning the flame sensor and checking the igniter does not resolve the lockout, or if you suspect a gas supply problem.

faq.q: Are Trane and American Standard the same?
faq.a: Trane and American Standard furnaces are made by the same manufacturer and share the same diagnostic codes and many of the same parts.

faq.q: How long does the lockout last?
faq.a: Typically 1-3 hours for auto-reset. You can manually reset by cycling the power switch.

faq.q: What is the most common cause?
faq.a: A dirty flame sensor is the most common cause of ignition lockout on Trane furnaces. Cleaning it takes only a few minutes.

title: Pressure Switch Stuck Open
shortDescription: The pressure switch did not close, indicating a draft or venting problem.
fullDescription: Two red flashes on a Trane furnace indicate the pressure switch failed to close after the draft inducer motor started. This safety device verifies proper exhaust venting before allowing gas to flow.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Never bypass the pressure switch. If you smell gas, evacuate and call your gas company.

causes.cause: Blocked exhaust or intake vent pipe
Cracked or kinked pressure switch hose
Weak or failing draft inducer motor

symptoms: Draft inducer runs but burners never light
Furnace shuts down after inducer startup
Humming from inducer without progression to ignition

fixSteps.title: Check Vent Pipes
fixSteps.instruction: Go outside and inspect exhaust and intake vent pipes for blockages from ice, snow, nests, or debris.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Pressure Hose
fixSteps.instruction: Check the small rubber hose from the pressure switch to the inducer. Ensure it is connected at both ends with no cracks or water inside.

fixSteps.title: Clear Condensate Drain
fixSteps.instruction: For 90%+ efficiency models, check the condensate drain and trap for clogs. Flush with warm water.

fixSteps.title: Test Pressure Switch
fixSteps.instruction: With inducer running, test for continuity across the pressure switch. It should show closed (continuity) when the inducer is operating.

whenToCallPro: If vents are clear, hose is good, and the error persists. The inducer motor or pressure switch may need replacement.

faq.q: Why does this code come up in winter?
faq.a: Ice and snow can block the outdoor exhaust/intake vents, especially during severe cold. Check them after storms.

faq.q: Can I jump the pressure switch?
faq.a: Never. The pressure switch ensures combustion gases are properly vented. Bypassing it risks CO poisoning.

faq.q: What does the pressure switch do?
faq.a: It verifies the draft inducer is creating enough airflow to safely exhaust combustion gases before allowing gas to flow to the burners.

title: Pressure Switch Stuck Closed
shortDescription: Pressure switch is closed before the draft inducer starts.
fullDescription: Three red flashes indicate the pressure switch is reading closed before the draft inducer motor has started. This could be a mechanically stuck switch, wiring fault, or water in the pressure hose.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: A stuck-closed pressure switch defeats a critical safety check. Do not operate the furnace until this is resolved.

causes.cause: Water trapped in pressure switch hose
Wiring short across pressure switch terminals

symptoms: Furnace will not start at all
Error appears immediately on heat call

fixSteps.title: Drain Pressure Hose
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the pressure switch hose and check for trapped water. Drain and reconnect.

fixSteps.title: Test Switch State
fixSteps.instruction: With power off and inducer not running, check continuity across the pressure switch. It should be open (no continuity).

fixSteps.title: Check Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect wires to the pressure switch for damage or shorts that could make the switch appear closed.

fixSteps.title: Replace Pressure Switch
fixSteps.instruction: If the switch tests as stuck closed, replace it with the correct part for your Trane model.

whenToCallPro: If you cannot determine why the switch is stuck or if replacement does not resolve the issue.

faq.q: Can condensation cause this?
faq.a: Yes, water from condensation can get trapped in the pressure switch hose and create a false closed reading.

faq.q: Is this dangerous?
faq.a: A stuck-closed pressure switch could theoretically allow the furnace to fire without proper venting. The control board detects this and prevents operation.

faq.q: How do I know which pressure switch to buy?
faq.a: Match the part number on the existing switch or use your Trane model number to find the correct replacement.

title: Open High Limit Switch — Overheating
shortDescription: The furnace has overheated and the high limit safety switch has tripped.
fullDescription: Four red flashes on a Trane furnace indicate the high temperature limit switch has opened due to overheating. This is typically caused by restricted airflow through the furnace.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Repeated overheating can crack the heat exchanger, which can lead to carbon monoxide leakage. Ensure CO detectors are functional. Do not ignore repeated limit switch trips.

causes.cause: Dirty evaporator coil (AC coil above furnace)

symptoms: Furnace starts but shuts off after a few minutes

fixSteps.title: Replace Air Filter
fixSteps.instruction: Check and replace the air filter immediately. This is the most common cause.

fixSteps.title: Open All Vents
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure all supply and return vents throughout the home are open and unobstructed.

fixSteps.title: Check Blower Motor
fixSteps.instruction: Set thermostat fan to ON. The blower should run. If not, the motor or capacitor may be faulty.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Evaporator Coil
fixSteps.instruction: If visible, check the evaporator coil above the furnace for heavy dust buildup that restricts airflow.

whenToCallPro: If the filter is clean, vents are open, and the furnace still overheats. The blower motor or heat exchanger needs inspection.

faq.q: Why does my Trane furnace keep overheating?
faq.a: The most common cause is a dirty air filter. Less common causes include a failing blower motor, dirty evaporator coil, or blocked ductwork.

faq.q: Can closing vents save energy?
faq.a: No, closing vents increases pressure and reduces airflow through the furnace, which can cause overheating. Keep all vents open.

faq.q: How serious is repeated overheating?
faq.a: Very serious. It can crack the heat exchanger over time, leading to expensive repairs and potential CO exposure.

title: Flame Sensed Without Gas Valve Command
shortDescription: Flame detected when the gas valve should be off — possible gas valve leak.
fullDescription: Five red flashes on a Trane furnace indicate flame is being detected when the gas valve has not been commanded to open. This is a critical safety condition.
safetyWarning: CRITICAL: This may indicate a leaking gas valve. If you smell gas, leave immediately and call 911 or your gas company. Do not operate any switches.

causes.cause: Flame sensor wiring shorted to ground

fixSteps.title: Check for Gas Odor
fixSteps.instruction: If you smell gas, leave the home immediately and call your gas company or 911.

fixSteps.title: Shut Down System
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the furnace power switch and close the gas shutoff valve.

fixSteps.title: Check Flame Sensor Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: With power off, inspect the flame sensor wire for damage or grounding against the chassis.

fixSteps.title: Call a Professional
fixSteps.instruction: This code requires professional diagnosis of the gas valve and flame sensing circuit.

whenToCallPro: Immediately. This is a safety-critical code.

faq.q: Is this an emergency?
faq.a: If you smell gas, yes. Even without a gas smell, have it inspected promptly as the gas valve may have an intermittent leak.

faq.q: Can I fix this myself?
faq.a: No. Gas valve issues must be diagnosed and repaired by a licensed HVAC technician.

faq.q: How much does a Trane gas valve cost?
faq.a: Parts cost $100-$300, with total repair typically $250-$700.

title: Power or Wiring Fault — Reversed Polarity
shortDescription: Line voltage polarity is reversed or ground is missing.
fullDescription: Six red flashes indicate the Trane furnace control board has detected reversed hot/neutral polarity or a grounding problem in the power supply wiring.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Turn off power at the breaker before checking any electrical connections.

causes.cause: Hot and neutral wires swapped at furnace
Polarity issue at circuit breaker panel

symptoms: Flame sensor may not detect flame

fixSteps.title: Kill Power
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the furnace breaker before any inspection.

fixSteps.title: Check Polarity
fixSteps.instruction: Use a voltage tester to verify hot and neutral are on the correct terminals at the furnace power connection.

fixSteps.title: Verify Ground
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the ground wire is securely attached to the furnace chassis.

fixSteps.title: Test Supply Circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Test the outlet or disconnect switch feeding the furnace for correct polarity and grounding.

whenToCallPro: If you are not comfortable with electrical wiring or if correcting polarity does not resolve the issue.

faq.q: Why does polarity matter?
faq.a: The flame sensing circuit needs proper polarity to work. Reversed polarity causes the flame sensor to fail.

faq.q: Is this a common problem?
faq.a: It is fairly common after new furnace installation or electrical work when wires get swapped.

faq.q: Can an outlet tester check this?
faq.a: Yes, an inexpensive outlet tester from the hardware store can verify polarity and grounding if the furnace is plug-connected.

title: Gas Valve Circuit Error
shortDescription: The control board has detected an abnormal condition in the gas valve wiring.
fullDescription: Seven red flashes on a Trane furnace indicate a fault in the gas valve circuit — either an open or shorted gas valve coil, or a wiring problem between the control board and the gas valve.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Do not attempt to bypass or manually operate the gas valve. If you smell gas, leave immediately and call your gas company.

causes.cause: Loose or damaged wiring to gas valve
Control board gas valve relay failure

symptoms: Igniter may glow but no gas flows
Furnace locked out during ignition sequence

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off power for 60 seconds and restore to check for transient fault.

fixSteps.title: Check Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: With power off, inspect the wire harness between the control board and gas valve for loose or damaged connections.

fixSteps.title: Test Gas Valve Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Measure resistance across the gas valve coil terminals. Compare to manufacturer specs.

fixSteps.title: Call Professional
fixSteps.instruction: Gas valve replacement requires a licensed technician who can properly test for leaks after installation.

whenToCallPro: For any gas valve replacement. This is not a DIY repair.

faq.q: Can I check the gas valve myself?
faq.a: You can check the wiring and coil resistance, but actual valve replacement must be done by a professional.

faq.q: How much is a Trane gas valve?
faq.a: The part costs $100-$300, with total installed cost of $250-$700.

faq.q: Could this just be a wiring issue?
faq.a: Yes, sometimes a loose connector is the only problem. Checking wiring is a reasonable first step.

title: Igniter Circuit Fault
shortDescription: The hot surface igniter circuit is open or has abnormal resistance.
fullDescription: Eight red flashes on a Trane furnace indicate the control board has detected a problem with the igniter circuit. The igniter is likely broken (open circuit) and cannot heat up to ignite the gas.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Turn off the furnace before working on the igniter. Do not touch the igniter element with bare hands.
symptoms: Draft inducer runs but ignition never begins

fixSteps.title: Inspect Igniter
fixSteps.instruction: Visually check for cracks in the igniter element. Even hairline cracks cause failure.

fixSteps.title: Check Connections
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the wire connector at the igniter is firmly seated.

fixSteps.title: Test Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect and measure igniter resistance. Good: 10-200 ohms. OL (open): broken.

fixSteps.title: Replace Igniter
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the mounting screw, swap the igniter. Handle the new one by the base only.

whenToCallPro: If replacing the igniter does not fix the issue, or if you are not comfortable accessing the burner compartment.

faq.q: How often do igniters fail?
faq.a: Typical lifespan is 3-7 years. Silicon nitride igniters last longer than silicon carbide.

faq.q: Can I use a universal igniter?
faq.a: Universal igniters may work but OEM Trane replacements ensure proper fit and performance.

faq.q: Why should I not touch the igniter?
faq.a: Skin oils create hot spots that cause premature cracking. Always handle by the ceramic base or use gloves.

title: Low Flame Signal / Weak Flame
shortDescription: The flame sensor is detecting a weak flame signal, causing intermittent shutdowns.
fullDescription: Nine red flashes on a Trane furnace indicate the flame sensor microamp reading is below the minimum threshold. The flame may be present but the sensor cannot adequately detect it, usually due to a dirty sensor or improper gas combustion.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Weak or improper combustion can produce carbon monoxide. Ensure CO detectors are working. If flames appear yellow or abnormal, turn off the furnace.
symptoms: Furnace lights but shuts off after a few seconds

fixSteps.title: Clean Flame Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and clean the flame sensor rod with fine emery cloth. This resolves the issue most of the time.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Sensor Insulator
fixSteps.instruction: Check the ceramic insulator for cracks. Replace the sensor if the insulator is damaged.

fixSteps.title: Observe Flame
fixSteps.instruction: Watch the burner flames. Blue with small yellow tips is normal. All yellow or lazy flames indicate combustion problems.

fixSteps.title: Check Gas Pressure
fixSteps.instruction: A technician can measure manifold gas pressure to ensure proper combustion.

whenToCallPro: If cleaning the flame sensor does not resolve the issue or if the flame color is abnormal.

faq.q: How do I find the flame sensor?
faq.a: The flame sensor is a thin metal rod mounted on a porcelain insulator, located in the burner assembly area. It has one wire attached.

faq.q: What is a good flame sensor reading?
faq.a: A healthy reading is typically 1.5-6 microamps. Below 1 microamp causes intermittent shutdowns.

faq.q: How often should I clean it?
faq.a: Annually during maintenance, or whenever the furnace starts shutting down shortly after ignition.

title: Rollout Limit Switch Open
shortDescription: The flame rollout safety switch has tripped, indicating flames escaping the combustion chamber.
fullDescription: Ten red flashes indicate the rollout limit switch has opened due to flames extending beyond the normal combustion area. This is a serious safety condition often caused by a blocked heat exchanger.
safetyWarning: CRITICAL: Flame rollout is extremely dangerous. It risks fire and carbon monoxide exposure. Turn off the furnace immediately and call a professional. Do not reset the rollout switch.

fixSteps.title: Shut Down Immediately
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the furnace power and gas supply valve right away.

fixSteps.title: Check CO Detectors
fixSteps.instruction: If CO detectors alarm, evacuate and call 911.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Exhaust Vent
fixSteps.instruction: Look for blockages in the exhaust vent but do not restart the furnace.

fixSteps.title: Call Professional
fixSteps.instruction: A licensed technician must inspect the heat exchanger before the furnace can be operated safely.

whenToCallPro: Immediately. Do not operate the furnace until professionally inspected.

faq.q: What causes flame rollout?
faq.a: Usually a blocked heat exchanger, blocked exhaust, or excessive gas pressure forcing flames out of the combustion chamber.

faq.q: Can I reset the rollout switch?
faq.a: Never reset it without professional inspection. The cause must be found and fixed first.

faq.q: Is a heat exchanger replacement worth it?
faq.a: Heat exchangers cost $1,000-$3,000+. For furnaces over 15 years old, full replacement is often more economical.

title: External Lockout / Power Reset Required
shortDescription: A continuous red LED indicates the system is in external lockout and requires a manual power reset.
fullDescription: A steady red LED (not blinking) on some Trane furnaces indicates an external lockout condition that requires cycling the furnace power to reset. The system will not auto-reset from this state.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: If you smell gas at any time, leave and call your gas company. If the lockout recurs after reset, do not keep resetting — call for service.

causes.cause: Repeated safety lockouts triggering hard lockout

symptoms: Will not start even when thermostat calls

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the furnace power switch or breaker. Wait 30-60 seconds. Restore power. The LED pattern after reset will indicate the underlying fault.

fixSteps.title: Watch for Error Code
fixSteps.instruction: After reset, watch the LED closely. If it shows a blink pattern before going to steady red again, note the number of blinks — that is the underlying fault code.

fixSteps.title: Address Root Cause
fixSteps.instruction: Use the blink code observed after reset to determine and fix the underlying issue before it causes another lockout.

fixSteps.title: Call Professional
fixSteps.instruction: If the furnace goes to steady red repeatedly, a professional needs to diagnose the recurring fault.

whenToCallPro: If the furnace repeatedly returns to a steady red lockout after being reset.

faq.q: How do I reset my Trane furnace?
faq.a: Turn off the power switch near the furnace (or the breaker), wait 30-60 seconds, then turn it back on.

faq.q: Why does it keep going to solid red?
faq.a: An underlying fault (like a bad igniter or pressure switch issue) is causing repeated lockouts. The root cause must be fixed.

faq.q: Is this an emergency?
faq.a: Not typically, unless you smell gas. It means the furnace has shut down safely but needs attention.

title: Normal Operation — Call for Heat Active
shortDescription: A steady green LED indicates the furnace is operating normally during a heating cycle.
fullDescription: On Trane furnaces with dual or multi-color LEDs, a steady green light indicates the system is in a normal heating cycle. The thermostat is calling for heat and the furnace is running properly.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Even during normal operation, maintain CO detectors and keep the furnace area clear.

causes.cause: If house is cold despite green light, check airflow

fixSteps.title: Confirm Heat Output
fixSteps.instruction: Check that warm air is coming from supply vents.

fixSteps.title: Check Filter
fixSteps.instruction: If airflow seems weak, check the air filter.

fixSteps.title: Verify Vent Positions
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure all supply and return vents are open.

fixSteps.title: Monitor Cycle Length
fixSteps.instruction: Normal cycles are 10-20 minutes. Very short cycles suggest a problem.

whenToCallPro: Only if the furnace runs but the home does not warm adequately.

faq.q: Is steady green an error?
faq.a: No, it means the furnace is running a normal heating cycle.

faq.q: How long should the green light stay on?
faq.a: The green light stays on during the heating cycle, typically 10-20 minutes per cycle.

faq.q: What if the green light is on but no heat?
faq.a: Check that the blower fan is running. If the inducer and burners are on but the blower is not, the blower motor or capacitor may be faulty.

title: No Power to Control Board
shortDescription: No LED visible indicates the control board has no power.
fullDescription: When no LED is illuminated on the Trane furnace control board, the board is not receiving power. Check the breaker, power switch, board fuse, and transformer.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Turn off the gas supply before troubleshooting electrical issues.

fixSteps.title: Check Breaker
fixSteps.instruction: Find and reset the furnace breaker if tripped.

fixSteps.title: Check Power Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the furnace power switch is in the ON position.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Board Fuse
fixSteps.instruction: Check the fuse on the control board. Replace if blown (match the amperage).

fixSteps.title: Test Transformer
fixSteps.instruction: Check for 24VAC output from the transformer. If no output with 120V input, the transformer has failed.

whenToCallPro: If the breaker trips repeatedly or the transformer has failed.

faq.q: Why does the breaker keep tripping?
faq.a: This usually indicates a short circuit in the furnace wiring, a failing blower motor, or a bad breaker. Have it inspected.

faq.q: What fuse does my Trane furnace use?
faq.a: Most use a 3A or 5A automotive-style fuse. Check the existing fuse or manual for the correct rating.

faq.q: Why does the fuse keep blowing?
faq.a: Repeated blown fuses usually indicate a short in the thermostat wiring or at the thermostat itself.
